threats.title: UNIv3 TVL Overcounting via Aggregate Pool Position Query Enables Share Price Inflation
threats.hypothesis_description: UNIv3Connector._getPositionTVL queries pool.positions(key) using keccak256(positionManager, tickLower, tickUpper), which returns the AGGREGATE liquidity for ALL NonfungiblePositionManager NFTs at those ticks, not just the vault's specific tokenId. Any external user's liquidity at the same tick range through the same positionManager inflates the vault's reported TVL. An attacker can deposit into the vault, add external liquidity at the same tick range, inflate TVL, then withdraw vault shares at the inflated price.
threats.investigation_rationale: The pool.positions(key) aggregation is a fundamental accounting error. The correct approach uses positionManager.positions(tokenId) for per-NFT liquidity. Since Uniswap V3 positions at popular tick ranges are common, this is always exploitable.

proof_of_signal.pattern: pool.positions(keccak256(positionManager, tL, tU)) returns aggregate liquidity, not per-tokenId liquidity

preconditions: Vault has at least one UNIv3 LP position
Attacker can add liquidity to same pool/tick range via the same NonfungiblePositionManager
AccountingManager uses TVL for share pricing

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: pool.positions(key) returns ALL liquidity from positionManager at ticks, not just vault's tokenId
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker adds external liquidity at same tick range to inflate vault TVL

economic_questions: What is the typical ratio of vault liquidity to total liquidity at a tick range?
How much capital is needed to significantly inflate the TVL?
Can the attack be done atomically with a flash loan?
validation_requirements: Verify pool.positions(key) vs positionManager.positions(tokenId) output difference
Calculate capital needed to double apparent TVL
Test end-to-end: deposit, inflate, withdraw cycle

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Equal to vault's UNI position size to 2x inflate (flash loanable)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Up to 50% of vault TVL in extreme case
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: High - attacker extracts from other depositors

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Flash loan for same-asset liquidity provision
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When vault has concentrated UNI v3 positions
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Low price impact - just adding/removing liquidity

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Pro-rata loss proportional to TVL inflation

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Quantify overcounting with real pool data

threats.title: Cross-Vault Privilege Escalation via Global FlashLoan Address Bypassing Per-Vault Access Control
threats.hypothesis_description: The registry.flashLoan() address is global (not per-vault) and grants onlyManager access to ALL vaults' connectors via NoyaGovernanceBase.onlyManager. In BalancerFlashLoan.receiveFlashLoan, the destinationConnector[] calls are not validated against the specified vaultId. A keeper for vault A can flash loan and make arbitrary calls to vault B's connectors, since those connectors see msg.sender == registry.flashLoan() and pass the onlyManager check for any vault.
threats.investigation_rationale: The global flashLoan address creates a systemic cross-vault trust boundary violation. Keeper compromise in one vault cascades to all vaults.

proof_of_signal.pattern: msg.sender == registry.flashLoan() passes onlyManager for ANY vaultId; flashLoan is global not per-vault

preconditions: Multiple vaults exist in the registry
BalancerFlashLoan is set as registry.flashLoan
Keeper for vault A is compromised or malicious

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: registry.flashLoan() is checked without vault scoping in onlyManager
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Keeper for vault A calls makeFlashLoan, passes vault B connector as destinationConnector
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Full manager access to all connectors across all vaults

economic_questions: Can vault A's keeper drain vault B's connector assets?
What is total TVL across all vaults at risk?
Are destinationConnector calls truly unrestricted?
validation_requirements: Construct POC: vault A keeper calls vault B connector via flash loan
Verify onlyManager passes for cross-vault calls
Map all manager-gated functions that could drain funds

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Up to total TVL of all other vaults

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Direct fund extraction, no market manipulation needed

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Verify flashLoan is global in Registry
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Trace msg.sender through flash loan to connector

threats.title: Lending Connector TVL Underflow Causes Protocol-Wide DoS on Underwater Positions
threats.hypothesis_description: Multiple connector _getPositionTVL implementations subtract debt from collateral without underflow protection (Solidity 0.8.x auto-reverts). If any lending position becomes underwater (debt > collateral due to interest accrual, liquidation, or market crash), the subtraction reverts. Since TVLHelper.getTVL iterates ALL positions, one underwater position reverts the entire vault TVL calculation, blocking all deposits, withdrawals, and fee calculations.
threats.investigation_rationale: Unchecked subtraction in _getPositionTVL across 5 connectors creates a systemic single-point-of-failure. Market volatility naturally creates underwater positions.

proof_of_signal.pattern: tvl = totalDepositAmount - totalBAmount; reverts if totalBAmount > totalDepositAmount

preconditions: Vault has at least one lending position (Silo, Prisma, Compound, Dolomite, or Aave)
Position becomes underwater (debt > collateral)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: totalCollateral - totalDebt without check; also return _getValue(collateral, base, collateralBalance) - _getValue(debTtoken, base, debtBalance) in PrismaConnector
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Natural market conditions or deliberate position manipulation causes underwater state
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Griefing attack: attacker borrows maximum, market moves against, vault is frozen. Or attacker profits from inability of others to withdraw.

economic_questions: How easily can a position go underwater in each protocol?
What is the cost to deliberately create an underwater position?
How long would the vault remain frozen?
validation_requirements: Test TVLHelper.getTVL with underwater position
Verify revert propagation to AccountingManager operations
Calculate capital needed to trigger underflow in each connector

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Cost of borrowing + potential liquidation loss
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Griefing: no direct profit; economic: trap others' funds
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Negative direct cost; systemic damage to vault
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Persistent until position is manually resolved

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All vault depositors unable to withdraw

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Check each connector's subtraction for underflow

threats.title: SiloConnector TVL Miscalculation via Hardcoded 18-Decimal Price Normalization
threats.hypothesis_description: SiloConnector._getPositionTVL normalizes prices using _getValue(asset, base, 1e18), assuming all tokens have 18 decimals. For USDC (6 decimals), this queries the price of 1e18 raw units (= 1e12 USDC = $1 trillion). The balance (in 6 decimal native units) is then multiplied by this inflated price and divided by 1e18, producing vastly incorrect TVL values. This leads to share price distortion enabling profit extraction.
threats.investigation_rationale: USDC is one of the most common DeFi tokens with 6 decimals. Deployment scripts show USDC as base token. Silo markets commonly include USDC. The miscalculation is mathematically provable.
deployment_assumptions: Silo positions involve non-18-decimal tokens (e.g., USDC, USDT, WBTC)
threats.config_evidence: deployAccountingManager.ts uses tokens['USDC'] as baseToken; USDC has 6 decimals

proof_of_signal.pattern: _getValue(assets[i], base, 1e18) hardcodes 1e18 regardless of token decimals; depositAmount * price / 1e18 produces wrong result for 6-decimal tokens

preconditions: SiloConnector holds positions in non-18-decimal tokens
USDC or USDT is used in Silo markets
Value oracle returns valid prices for the requested amounts

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: For USDC: price = _getValue(USDC, base, 1e18) gets price of 1e12 USDC; balance(1000e6) * price / 1e18 is wildly wrong
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Automatic miscalculation inflates or deflates TVL for non-18-decimal tokens
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: If TVL is inflated, existing depositors extract excess value; if deflated, new depositors get shares too cheaply

economic_questions: What is the exact TVL distortion factor for USDC (6 decimals)?
Does the oracle handle 1e18 units of a 6-decimal token correctly or revert?
Which direction does the error favor (inflation or deflation)?
validation_requirements: Calculate exact TVL output for 1000 USDC deposit in Silo
Compare against correct TVL calculation
Test with actual oracle behavior for extreme amounts

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Proportional to decimal mismatch (up to 1e12x error for 6-decimal tokens)

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Always present when non-18-decimal tokens are used

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Depends on TVL distortion direction and magnitude

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Token decimals for each Silo market asset

threats.title: Holding Position Index Corruption via Swap-and-Pop Key Mismatch Breaks Vault Accounting
threats.hypothesis_description: When removing a holding position via swap-and-pop, the isPositionUsed mapping key for the moved element is reconstructed using calculatorConnector (struct field 0). But the original key was created using msg.sender (the calling connector). For token positions (type 0), calculatorConnector is the accountingManager while the creator is the connector—these differ. After any swap-and-pop involving token positions, the index mapping becomes corrupted, causing ghost entries and preventing proper position management.
threats.investigation_rationale: Token position creation/removal is the most common registry operation. Every connector calls _updateTokenInRegistry which creates token positions. Any removal that triggers swap-and-pop corrupts the mapping for moved token positions.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Swap-and-pop uses calculatorConnector for key reconstruction: keccak256(holdingPositions[positionIndex].calculatorConnector, ...) but creation used keccak256(msg.sender, ...)

preconditions: Vault has multiple holding positions including token positions
A position removal triggers swap-and-pop on a token position
calculatorConnector != creating connector for the swapped position

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Creation key: keccak256(msg.sender, positionId, data) vs reconstruction key: keccak256(calculatorConnector, positionId, data)
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Normal operation (remove any position) corrupts other token position indices
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Corrupted indices lead to duplicate positions, eventually hitting maxNumHoldingPositions limit, freezing the vault

economic_questions: How quickly does index corruption accumulate?
Can corrupted indices lead to TVL miscounting?
Does corruption lead to vault DoS via maxNumHoldingPositions?
validation_requirements: Create multiple token positions from different connectors
Remove a non-token position triggering swap-and-pop
Verify isPositionUsed mapping corruption

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Vault DoS or accounting errors enabling extraction

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Vault DoS or incorrect share pricing

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Map all token position creation paths
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Trace key divergence for token positions

threats.title: Registry onlyVaultMaintainer Modifier Uses Wrong Boolean Logic, Blocking Vault Administration
threats.hypothesis_description: The onlyVaultMaintainer modifier uses || (OR) in its revert condition instead of && (AND), requiring callers to be BOTH the vault maintainer AND hold EMERGENCY_ROLE. This locks out the intended maintainer (likely a timelock) from calling addConnector, removeTrustedPosition, and updateConnectorTrustedTokens unless it also has the EMERGENCY_ROLE. The same bug exists in onlyVaultMaintainerWithoutTimeLock, blocking addTrustedPosition.
threats.investigation_rationale: Comparing with onlyVaultGoverner which correctly uses &&, this is clearly a code bug. Critical vault administration functions become inaccessible, potentially bricking vault management.
threats.config_evidence: Deployment scripts (updateRegistry.ts) show the same address used for all roles, masking the bug. In production with separate addresses, maintainer functions would be inaccessible.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (msg.sender != vaults[_vaultId].maintainer || hasRole(EMERGENCY_ROLE, msg.sender) == false) revert; should use && not ||

preconditions: Vault maintainer and emergency addresses are different (standard production config)
Maintainer attempts to call addConnector, removeTrustedPosition, updateConnectorTrustedTokens, or addTrustedPosition

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: || in revert means BOTH conditions must be false to pass; requires sender to be maintainer AND have EMERGENCY_ROLE
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Not an attack but a governance lockout—vaults cannot be administered
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Funds become stuck if connectors cannot be added/removed; positions cannot be updated

economic_questions: Are production deployments using the same address for all roles?
Can emergency role holder unilaterally manage vaults?
What is the recovery path if vault management is locked?
validation_requirements: Call addConnector as maintainer without EMERGENCY_ROLE - should revert
Compare with onlyVaultGoverner which uses correct && logic
Test all functions gated by both buggy modifiers

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Present from deployment with separate role addresses

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Funds locked due to inability to manage vault

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Check deployment addresses for role separation
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Boolean logic analysis of modifier conditions

threats.title: Disabled Swap/Bridge Routes Remain Executable Due to onlyExistingRoute AND Logic Bug
threats.hypothesis_description: The onlyExistingRoute modifier uses AND logic: `if (routes[_routeId].route == address(0) && !routes[_routeId].isEnabled) revert`. A route with a non-zero address but isEnabled=false passes this check. Since executeSwap and executeBridge never independently verify isEnabled, emergency-disabled routes can still be used, defeating the setEnableRoute safety mechanism.
threats.investigation_rationale: setEnableRoute is the emergency mechanism to disable compromised routes. If it doesn't work, compromised LiFi routes or deprecated bridges remain usable, enabling fund extraction through malicious swap parameters.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (routes[_routeId].route == address(0) && !routes[_routeId].isEnabled) revert; should use || not &&

preconditions: A route has been disabled via setEnableRoute(routeId, false)
Route has a non-zero address (which all real routes do)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: AND condition means only routes that are BOTH address(0) AND disabled are blocked
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Use a disabled route to execute swaps/bridges through compromised implementation
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: If route was disabled due to vulnerability, attacker can still exploit it

economic_questions: What happens when a compromised LiFi route is used?
Can the swap implementation steal tokens via malicious calldata?
Is slippage protection still enforced on disabled routes?
validation_requirements: Disable a route and verify executeSwap still works
Test with isEnabled=false and route != address(0)

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Up to full swap amount if route is malicious

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Direct fund theft via compromised route

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Vault depositors whose funds are swapped through compromised route

threats.title: DolomiteConnector openBorrowPosition Removes Position Immediately Due to Incorrect removePosition Flag
threats.hypothesis_description: DolomiteConnector.openBorrowPosition calls registry.updateHoldingPosition with removePosition=true when it should be false. This means new borrow positions are immediately removed from registry tracking. TVL calculations will miss these borrow positions entirely, causing the vault to underreport debt and overreport net TVL, inflating share prices.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a clear code bug (true vs false parameter). Since debt isn't tracked, TVL = collateral only, making shares appear more valuable than they are.

proof_of_signal.pattern: registry.updateHoldingPosition(vaultId, ..., abi.encode(accountId), '', true) passes true (remove) instead of false (add)

preconditions: Vault uses DolomiteConnector
Manager opens a borrow position via openBorrowPosition

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: removePosition=true on position creation; borrow position never tracked in registry
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: TVL automatically inflated because debt from borrow positions is untracked
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Existing depositors can withdraw more than their fair share; new depositors overpay for shares

economic_questions: How much TVL inflation occurs from untracked borrow positions?
Does _getPositionTVL also miss the borrow account?
Can this be exploited to extract value?
validation_requirements: Open borrow position and verify it's not in holdingPositions
Calculate TVL with and without tracked borrow position

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Proportional to borrow size vs TVL

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Any time Dolomite borrow positions are used

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Trace position through registry after openBorrowPosition

threats.title: SNXv3Connector Double-Counts Delegated Collateral in TVL Calculation
threats.hypothesis_description: SNXV3Connector._getPositionTVL computes TVL as _getValue(collateralType, base, totalDeposited + totalAssigned). In SNX V3, totalDeposited typically includes totalAssigned (assigned is a subset of deposited). Adding them together double-counts delegated collateral, inflating the reported TVL and share price.
threats.investigation_rationale: SNX V3 getAccountCollateral returns (totalDeposited, totalAssigned, totalLocked) where totalAssigned is collateral delegated to pools (a subset of totalDeposited). Adding them is a clear accounting error.

proof_of_signal.pattern: tvl = _getValue(collateralType, base, totalDeposited + totalAssigned); totalAssigned is subset of totalDeposited

preconditions: Vault has SNX V3 positions with delegated collateral
totalAssigned > 0 (collateral delegated to pools)

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: TVL automatically inflated by totalAssigned amount
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Share price inflated by up to 2x the actual collateral value

economic_questions: Does SNX V3 totalDeposited include totalAssigned?
What fraction of deposits are typically assigned?
How much TVL inflation does this produce?
validation_requirements: Query SNX V3 getAccountCollateral and verify relationship between fields
Calculate TVL error for typical delegation ratios

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Up to 2x TVL inflation for fully delegated positions

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Always present when collateral is delegated

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: New vault depositors overpay for shares
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Proportional to SNX position size vs total TVL

threats.title: Cross-Chain TVL Update Lacks Timestamp Monotonicity Check Enabling Stale Data Injection
threats.hypothesis_description: OmnichainManagerBaseChain.updateTVL accepts any updateTime without checking if it's newer than the existing positionTimestamp. If LayerZero delivers messages out of order, a stale TVL value can overwrite a more recent one. An attacker could exploit windows where stale (lower) TVL is recorded to deposit at cheap share prices, then trigger a fresh TVL update to profit from the correction.
threats.investigation_rationale: LayerZero does not guarantee in-order delivery. Without monotonicity checks, the most recent TVL data can be replaced by older data, creating share price manipulation windows.

proof_of_signal.pattern: updateTVL accepts any updateTime; no check that updateTime > existing positionTimestamp

preconditions: Vault operates across multiple chains
LayerZero delivers TVL update messages out of order
Attacker can monitor and time deposits around TVL updates

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: registry.updateHoldingPostionWithTime called without comparing updateTime to current timestamp
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Stale (lower) TVL overwrites current TVL; attacker deposits at deflated share price

economic_questions: How often does LayerZero deliver out of order?
How much TVL variance exists between updates?
Can an attacker force out-of-order delivery?
validation_requirements: Verify no monotonicity check in updateTVL
Simulate out-of-order message delivery scenario
Calculate profit from TVL desync

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Large deposit to capture TVL spread

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Dilution proportional to stale TVL gap

threats.title: Empty Watchers.verifyRemoveLiquidity Provides Zero Withdrawal Validation
threats.hypothesis_description: Watchers.verifyRemoveLiquidity has an empty function body, providing no validation of withdrawal parameters. BaseConnector.sendTokensToTrustedAddress calls this function when the accountingManager requests token transfers, but since it does nothing, any amount passes verification. The watcher security layer, designed as a safety net against malicious fund extraction, is completely absent.
threats.investigation_rationale: The function receives withdrawAmount, sentAmount, and data but performs no checks. Any token transfer requested by the accountingManager automatically succeeds. This removes the intended defense-in-depth for fund withdrawals.

proof_of_signal.pattern: function verifyRemoveLiquidity(uint256, uint256, bytes memory) external view { } — empty body, no validation

preconditions: AccountingManager calls sendTokensToTrustedAddress on a connector
The watcher is supposed to validate withdrawal legitimacy

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: verifyRemoveLiquidity receives amount parameters but does nothing with them
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Any withdrawal amount passes verification; no safety check on fund extraction
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: If accountingManager is buggy or compromised, arbitrary amounts can be extracted from connectors

economic_questions: What validation was the watcher intended to perform?
Can the accountingManager extract more than it should?
Does the newAmount in data ever exceed the requested amount?
validation_requirements: Verify the empty function body
Test withdrawal with mismatched amounts
Analyze accountingManager withdrawal logic

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Up to total loss of connector assets

threats.title: BalancerFlashLoan Allows Arbitrary Unvalidated Calls to Non-Connector Contracts
threats.hypothesis_description: In BalancerFlashLoan.receiveFlashLoan, the destinationConnector[] addresses are called with arbitrary calldata without validating them against the registry. While the receiver is checked, the execution targets can be any contract. A compromised keeper multisig could use this to call arbitrary contracts (including vault connectors for other vaults, token contracts for approvals, or external protocols) through the flash loan mechanism.
threats.investigation_rationale: The receiver validation is present, but destinationConnector validation is absent. This expands the attack surface beyond registered connectors. Combined with the global flashLoan address granting manager access, this creates a wide-open execution path.

proof_of_signal.pattern: destinationConnector[i].call{gas: gas[i]}(callingData[i]) — no registry validation on destinationConnector addresses

preconditions: Keeper multisig is compromised or colluding
BalancerFlashLoan is the registered flashLoan contract
Attacker crafts malicious destinationConnector and callingData

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: No validation: registry.isAnActiveConnector only checked for receiver, not destinationConnector
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Keeper constructs flash loan with arbitrary call targets
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Full protocol funds at risk via arbitrary contract interactions

economic_questions: What contracts could a malicious destinationConnector target?
Can token approvals be set to attacker addresses?
What is the blast radius of arbitrary call capability?
validation_requirements: Verify no destinationConnector validation exists
Test calling non-connector contract as destinationConnector
Map potential malicious call paths
